source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Identify and name common animals including fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals; compare their structure; identify basic human body parts and link them to the senses.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack uses observation-first picture and audio tasks to identify animal groups, compare visible body structures and connect human sense organs to simple observations.

objective.child_goal: I can name animal groups, compare bodies and use my senses safely.
objective.statement: Identify and group common animals, compare simple body structures, and name basic human body parts associated with the five senses.
misconceptions: Groups animals only by where they are seen, so a whale is called a fish because it lives in water.
Thinks every animal has the same body parts and covering as a human.
Uses one striking feature, such as wings, without checking other observable features.
Links a sense to the object being noticed rather than the body part used to notice it.
Thinks humans are separate from animals rather than mammals.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A living thing that needs food and can sense what is around it.
vocabulary.audio_script: An animal is a living thing. Humans are animals too.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A child, robin, frog, fish and cat shown in one animal family frame.

vocabulary.child_explanation: An animal group that includes humans, dogs, bats and whales.
vocabulary.audio_script: Humans, dogs, bats and whales are mammals.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A human, dog, bat and whale card sharing a mammal badge.

vocabulary.child_explanation: One part of an animal's body, such as an eye, leg, wing or fin.
vocabulary.audio_script: Body parts help animals move, eat and sense their surroundings.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Simple animal outlines with large eye, leg, wing and fin hotspots.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A way the body finds out what is around us.
vocabulary.audio_script: Our senses help us notice light, sound, smell, taste and touch.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Five simple icons linked to eyes, ears, nose, tongue and skin.

vocabulary.audio_script: Scientists observe carefully before they decide.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A calm magnifier framing one visible animal feature.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Tap one body part you can see.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Establish careful observation before naming or grouping an animal.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Look closely. What body part can you see?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: One large, uncluttered animal illustration with generous feature hotspots.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: These animals look different, but they all belong to the animal family.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Broaden the animal concept to include humans and varied animal groups.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A fish, a bird, a frog and a human are all animals.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Five cards enter one broad animal-family frame without ranking or competition.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: A whale lives in water, but it is a mammal, not a fish.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model grouping from several relevant features rather than habitat alone.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Where an animal lives is one clue, but it is not the whole answer. A whale is a mammal.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Whale and fish comparison cards with fins, body shape and group badges; no anatomy beyond Year 1 need.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort the animals, then tap a body feature that helped you.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Practise grouping common animals and justifying choices with observable structure.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Choose a group, then show the feature you noticed.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Large animal cards, audio-labelled group mats and optional feature hotspots.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which body part helps you hear the bell?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check links between basic human body parts and senses in a familiar safe context.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Think about the body part you use to notice a sound.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A child outline with eyes, ears and nose as large choices; bell shown as a context cue.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Pip why a human belongs in the animal family.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair the human-versus-animal misconception through age-appropriate comparison.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I thought humans were not animals. What can we observe that helps us understand?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Human and other mammal cards with shared body-part icons and a mammal group frame.

manipulatives.purpose: Support naming, grouping and comparison using clear animal images and observable features.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Children tap or move animal cards to audio-labelled group mats, then select a feature hotspot as evidence.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Large cards, single-tap placement, keyboard and switch scanning, spoken labels, high-contrast outlines and no dependence on colour.

manipulatives.purpose: Connect eyes, ears, nose, tongue and skin with sight, hearing, smell, taste and touch.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: A body-part choice links to one familiar observation; children may tap, use keys or answer orally to an adult.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: No requirement to taste, smell or touch real substances; alternatives avoid sensory discomfort, allergies and medical assumptions.

practice.interleaving_notes: Start with familiar named examples and obvious visible features. Add less familiar examples and cross-habitat contrasts only after broad groups and body-part language are stable.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Identify familiar animals and notice one clear visible body feature before grouping.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: large animal image + spoken name or clue + 2-3 feature hotspots + replay
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use anatomically clear, child-friendly images; avoid frightening poses, injury, predation or misleading cartoon anatomy.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Group common fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals using more than habitat alone.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: 3-5 animal cards + two or three audio-labelled group mats + optional feature evidence
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Teacher review must reject ambiguous life stages, unusual exceptions and examples whose group cannot be inferred at Year 1 level.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Compare common animal body structures including limbs, fins, wings, tails and coverings.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: paired animals + spoken compare prompt + large feature hotspots + same/different response
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep claims observable and simple; do not imply that all members of a group share every pictured feature.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Link basic human body parts to sight, hearing, smell, taste and touch through familiar observations.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: spoken observation + body-part choices + optional body outline hotspot
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use inclusive language for sensory differences and accept assistive-device contexts; never require real sensory exposure.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Retrieve animal groups and apply observation language to new but age-appropriate examples.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: spaced review queue + one familiar and one new example + group choice + because-feature prompt
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Oral, pointing and symbol-supported justifications must carry equal evidence value where appropriate.

body.prompt: A whale lives in water. Which animal group does it belong to?

hints: Where it lives is not the only clue.
A whale belongs with other mammals.
question_variants.explanation: A whale is a mammal even though it lives in water. Habitat alone does not decide an animal group.

body.prompt: Tap a body covering that helps you identify the robin as a bird.

hints: Look at what covers the robin's body.
question_variants.explanation: Feathers are an observable body covering that identifies the robin as a bird.

body.prompt: A bell rings. Which body part helps you hear it?

expected_answer.inclusive_note: Accept supported communication showing the ear/hearing link.

hints: Think about the body part linked to hearing.
Your ears help you notice sounds.
question_variants.explanation: Ears are the body parts associated with the sense of hearing.

body.prompt: Put the human card into the correct broad animal group.

hints: Humans belong to the mammal group.
question_variants.explanation: Humans are animals, and the animal group humans belong to is mammals.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Groups animals only by where they are seen, so a whale is called a fish because it lives in water.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child sorts whale, dolphin or seal as fish and explains only that it lives in water.
repair_sequence: Acknowledge habitat as one useful observation.
Compare the whale with a fish using two teacher-selected group clues and the correct group labels.
Sort a new water-living mammal and a fish while saying that habitat alone is not enough.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child groups a new common aquatic mammal correctly without relying only on habitat.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks every animal has the same body parts and covering as a human.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child assigns arms, skin or human-style features to fish, birds or reptiles.
repair_sequence: Place a human and one contrasting animal side by side.
Tap one shared feature and one different feature.
Describe the animal using its visible body parts before naming its group.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child identifies one accurate similarity and one difference between a human and another animal.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Uses one striking feature, such as wings, without checking other observable features.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child groups a bat as a bird because it has wings.
repair_sequence: Keep the noticed feature visible and praise the observation.
Add another relevant clue and compare the bat with a familiar bird.
Ask which animal group each belongs to and state that one feature may not be enough.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child uses more than one clue when grouping a new comparison pair.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Links a sense to the object being noticed rather than the body part used to notice it.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child answers bell, flower or food when asked which body part supports a sense.
repair_sequence: Separate the object card from the body outline.
Name the observation, then trace a simple line to the relevant body part.
Repeat with a different safe context for the same sense.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child matches a new observation to the associated body part.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks humans are separate from animals rather than mammals.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The child leaves a human card outside every animal group.
repair_sequence: Place humans inside the broad animal-family frame.
Compare simple shared needs and body features without suggesting all animals are alike.
Move the human card into the mammal group beside familiar mammals.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The child identifies a human as an animal and a mammal in a new sort.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use still animal images, optional environmental sound, plain backgrounds and no sudden animal movement or loud calls.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace card travel, scanner sweeps and sense paths with static outlines and instant selected states.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read animal names, group labels, prompts and explanations aloud with replay and visible playback controls.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use clear pictures, body icons and spoken labels; written text remains short and is never the only source of a clue.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Show one animal or comparison pair at a time and limit early choices to three.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow repeated observation, audio replay, extra response time and a feature-by-feature reveal.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Praise accurate observations even when the group choice needs repair, then invite a second look without red failure states.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach animal, human, body, fish, bird, mammal, eye, ear, nose and sense using pictures and gestures.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires identification and broad grouping of familiar animals plus correct body-part-to-sense links; secure requires feature-based comparison and transfer to new age-appropriate examples after spaced review.
evidence.parent_summary: Can name and group common animals, compare visible body parts, explain that humans are mammals and link body parts to the senses.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Uses observable features rather than habitat alone to group animals, compares simple structures and identifies eyes, ears, nose, tongue and skin in sense contexts.
next_actions: Revisit uncertain groups with familiar local examples and clear images.
Use safe outdoor observation without touching or approaching animals.
Connect animal structures to simple needs and habitats in later science work.

qa.notes: Deep review sample for the first balanced Year 1 wave. Requires science-teacher review of examples and exceptions, inclusive SEND review, image and audio review, safeguarding review and pilot calibration before promotion.
